CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Arnott’s ““ Times ' is quite interested in the Irish

Volunteers. The Irish Volunteers gave the British
Goyernment such 4 spasm that the Arms Proclamation
came hot foot after them. Owing to the Larkinite
tactics at their founding in Dublin and the litble row in
Cork the Volunteers ul)penred to be good copy to such
a paper as Amnott’s ‘' Times.” And so the paper
boomed them, and did not boycott them as it boycotted
~ the G.A.A. and the great hurling and Gaelic football
inatches. It will shortly be as loth to mention them ag
ackle Messrs. Gill & Son. We think the atten-
en to the Irish Volunteers by Arnott’s paper
\rdilaun’s *‘ Mail "’ has been a good and timely
t. The leading article of Friday last in

? is so far from the realities, so full

ed contempt of the Irish Party, and

80 bordering on the hysterical that it might have been
written by Plunkett’s man, the Hairy Ifairy. But it
is not what is in the article that counts, it is the value,
just at the initial stages, of the advertisement of the
Irish Volunteers that counts.

To anyone who knows Ireland there is no secret to be
read in the Irish Volunteers. We Irish are a martial
people, but for generations we never had a chance to
indulge our martial spirit in a regular and ordered way.
We were free to take the shilling and join the British
army, and even though it was partly only.the scum of
e population who did join, it must be said that as
soldiers, apart from what or who they were fighting for,
hey rather tended to uphold the martial character of
the sh people.” But the boys of Ireland for many
generations passed into middle age without any chance
of indulging their national martial spirit. ~ Dublin
Casfle and all the forces of the British power of Coer-
cion saw to that. Now there is nothing more secret in
the Irish Volunteer movement than there is in the fact
that a spiral spring will rise if the pressure that kept it
down is taken off. The accident of Carson’s Wooden
Tusketeers, the winking at them of the Government,
and the encouragement of them by the British Opposi-
tion took off the external pressure that forbade the rise
the Trish Volunteers for generations. And the Volun-
teers rose. We believe the LEADER was actually the
first to suggest that the way was clear for them. Bub
we have no doubt that the situation was so obvious
that it must have been plain to many observant Irish-
men. Carson’s Wooden Musketeers, the ridiculous rear-
guard of the contemptible anti-Irish Carrion Crows,
having been permitted, it was curious to see how the
Governmenl could veto the Irish Volunteers. As a
matter of fact the Government promptly replied by
their Proclamation about the importation of arms. But
they have not yet aimed at the drilling of the Irish
Volunteers by suppressing the drilling of Carson’s
squads; that may come. L

Carson’s Wooden Musketeers gave a chance to the
Trish Volunteers, and the Irish Volunteers rushed into
evidence. That is the whole fact and all the
secret.  The Irish Volunteers are not ouf ne-
cessarily to fight anyone; they are the visible and out-
ward sign of the love of martial life that is a strong and
fairly general mark of Irish youth. ~We could con-
ceive the young men of Ireland in their hundreds of
thousands flocking to the colours of the Irish Volun-
teers, drilling, marching, rifle practising, camping out,
manceuvring and falling out in due course into mature
citizenship without ever having fought jan enemy.
Volunteering for young Irishmen of martial spivit is
good in itself, and it is because it is so, and because
young TIreland so largely possesses the martial spirit
that young Ireland is flocking in its thousands into the
ranks of the Irish Volunteers.

That is the head, front and centre of it all. Of -
course, odd busy-bodies and cranks may air them-
selves otherwise about the matter; and it is possible
that an extreme mouthy section might attempt a little
exploitation. These are the only risks that we see, and
they are small. We might say that Irish Volunteers
will be-popular as steeplechasing is popular in Ireland;
unlike various journalistic ventures, Irish Volunteering
is really a long-felt want. ¢ R

We attempted to make the Christmas Number of
the LEADER rather ‘s Christmas Annual this year. It
Was current and up to date-—sa much so that the Post
Office did not dare to'challenge its right to pass on a
ha’penny inland postage—but it is turrent for the
season rather' than for the week. The sale of it still
proceeds; if any of our readers wanting extra copies
find their newsagents sold out we have a small number
of copies on hand, and a copy may be still had'at 23d.
post free. The *‘ Cork Examiner’ says: “ The out-
standing feature of the Christmas Number of Tnr
LEADER, published on the 18th inst., is a series of pic-
tures, ten in number, showing various people ‘ out for
this, that and the other thing, from revolution down-
wards, in Ireland at the present day. These, how-
ever, by no means exhaust the special attractions of the
number, for besides pungent notes on current affairs,
there are articles in verse as well as prose, on popular
topics and prominent personages, in which a great
deal of good-humoured banter conceals a more serious
purpose, or perhaps it might be said sugar-coats the
pill. There are also several cartoons and other draw-
ings, and both letterpress and pictures are extremely
clever. The price is only twopence.”

CATHLEEN NI HOULIHAN.

Who art thou that comest forth from an Island of the
North,
On thy hands the gyves of thraldom, in thy heart
@ bloody sword?
In what wisec didst thow sin, that they will not let
thee in
To the Council of the Nations and the peace of their
accord ?

’Twas my sin, 'twas my shame, that T kept an
honoured name,
When the Nations broke the oath that their father’s
fathers swore,
When they rent the Seamless Garb, when they plunged
the pitiless barb
In the heart of the mother who suckled them of
yore.

Who art thou that comest down from an Island of
renown,
With thy loosened hair about thee and thy comely
vesture torn?
Say, what agonising fears have drenched thy eyes in
tears,
Say, what hands have rent thy raiment, and crowned
thee with a thorn?

The hands that soiled my gown, and the hands that
made my crown, .
Were the Tyrant’s hands of blood and the Traitor's
hands of guile.
From my haggard bosom bare they cast forth my
daughters fair, 3 )
And they marched my sons to serve them in their
drunken rank-and-file. -

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 27, 1913.

0 thou that comest forth from the Island of the North,
With the shades of Death about thee, and thy wan
lips tinged with gall, <
Is there no surcease of woe, must thy stumbling foot-
steps go g 3
Through the darkening Vale of Weeping, untit the
end of all?

By the mercy of our God (who makes . green the
withered sod, ;
And draws out a distilled essence from the chaos of
decay) Ao
The vanguard of the Night has wheeled about in flight,
And I hear the gathering tumults of the Trumpets of
the Day ! g

Trom the wreck of vanished years, from the mire of
blood and tears, .
TFrom the mound of broken hearts, from the gashes
of the sword,
There will rise, there will rise to the vault of the skies
A Senate for the Nation and a Temple to the Lord.

I'rom the bitter, bitter crust, from the ashes and the
dust
There will spring the Nation’s manhood, and my
wounds will not be vain;
And my children that stand forth in the Island of the
North
Will walk like Sons of Thunder—a Nation once
again | -
J. R. MEAGHER.

The Dublin ““ Stop Press '’ frick is an old trick,
and we are seldom caught by it. A friend of ours last
Saturday bought a ““Stop Press’’ of Ardilaun’s ‘“‘Mail.”
This Saturday “ Stop Press’’ of Ardilaun’s “ Mail *’
contained three -lines about the Labour Conference.
Our friend showed us a copy of this Saturday ‘* Stop
Press '’ of ““ Buft.”” We keep an eye on * Buff *’ just
as we do on the Westmoreland Street Bluffer. There
was an editorial article in it, and we thought that the
heading of the article was a thing that we had seen
before. Unlike Scotland Yard detectives we had a real
clue. We subsequently discovered that the heading
of each page of this Satwrday ‘‘ Stop Press’’ of Ardi-
laun’s *“ Mail read thus :—*‘ Dublin Evening Mail Day,
December, 1918.”  The trickster had chipped off
“Fri” and the date ‘“19th.”’  Originally the head-
ing was ‘‘Dublin Evening Mail, Friday, December
Ipbh, 1913,”” but the ““Fri’’ and the ‘“ 19th ”’ were
simply chipped oft in order to turn a Friday, or rather
part of a Iriday, evening paper into a Saturday * Stop
Press ’!  This is about the lowest and meanest thing
in the * Stop Press’’ humbug that we have yet
encountered.

Arott’s < Times’’ is a bigot and a coward. This is no

news to our readers, of cowrse, but there are many
silly” people who probably have not found it out yet.
Messrs. M. H. Gill & Son, Ltd., the well-known
Catholic and Irish publishers and booksellers, had occa-
sion to call upon one of their staff who happened to be
a Protestant to resign his position. The fact that a
Protestant held a responsible position in Messrs. Gill's
firm for nearly forty years is significant of the tolerance
of that firm.  If they called upon him to resign after
s0 many years’ service it can at least be said that there
are a large number of Protestant firms in Ireland who
could not call upon a Catholic employee in a respon-
sible position to resign for the very good reason that
they rnake sure that such persons don’t get responsible
_positions in their service.

Well, as soon as Messrs. Gill, in the exercise of this
legal right, dispensed with the services of one of their
staff who happened to be a Protestant, Amott’s
“Pimes”’ and Ardilaun’s ‘‘Express’’—Ardilaun’s ha’-
penny Mail out of respect for ““Idolaters’ ’’ ha'pennies
keeps its anti-Catholic and anti-Tvish spleen under con-
siderable restraint—came out like wild Indians in a war
dance. It is only fair to state that we thought they
would by their onslaught frighten Messrs. Gill & Son.

The man whose services Messrs: Gill dispensed with
does ot interest us, and we see that a collection is
being raised for him. What interests us is the surprise
that the miserable bigot of Westmoreland Street: must
have got when it found that it was not a rabbit it-had
attacked but a lion!  Arnott’s *“ Times ' led off with a
leading article, and published several anonymous letters
against Messrs. Gill, though the mean and cowardly
fused to publish two letters in sclf-defence sent
) . Gill, Messrs. Gill run a monthly periodi-

that the two letters sent by Messrs. Gill in reply to
all these disgraceful imputations, calenlated to bring
public odium on their house, were pressed by the
* Irish Times " alone among the morning newspapers of

 Irish Times ’ not only suppressed these letters, but
refused even to acknowledge a letter of complaint ad-
dressed him personally by Messrs. Gill, as he has re-
fused to acknowledge the first edition of the December
number of the ‘ Catholic: Bullétin’ containing a full

man  who, while defaming a popular and long-
established firm under cover of a maze of irrelevancies,
had the brass to say: * the one thing we hope that Mr.
Brady will not do is to take refuge in silence.” e has

no apology to pampered bigotry for being alive to-day,
and about our business. We are not to be intimidated

the ‘ Irish Times ' and others may do we do not intend
‘ to take refuge in silence.” We propose, on the con-
trary, to * exer our ‘ religious and civil rights ' until
the ‘Irish Times’ has been shown up in Dublin and
Ireland and outside of Treland for the cowardly and
contemptible paper it actually onduct in this
matter we regret to have to s nothing less
than journalistic blackguardism ask its readers
and owrs to take note of it. In its own words, the one
thing we hope the * Irish Times’ will not do now is take
refuge in si{cncc."

Arnott’s editor must have been staggered when he
found what he was up agai He is now knocked
into silence and the suppressing of two letters from
. Gill. Of course it is not news to us that the
er of Canon MacInerney is a coward, bub some

The ¢ Irish Times,’” indeed, is a low and a mean paper.

BEFORE THE DAWN.

Scene—College Green at midnight.
(Enter Cathleen Ni Houlihan.)

Cath. (singing).—
When Unionist bigots the nation were ruling,
Of howling green faction was heard not a sound,
No crisis arose out of Socialist fooling,
-And freak agitation was not to be found.
"Tig only since friendly Home Rulers took station
To help on the land which the Tories befoul,
That all the tame bounders and quacks of the nation
0f freakish injustice do clamour and howl.

But there is no danger such cranky diversion
Will back to the Castle the Tories bring o'er,
The freaks to reward with a reign of coercion,
And redistribution impose on our shore.
A vision of freedom from Unionist duffers,
With College Green Senate before me unfold,
Where Socialists, cranks, Hairy Fairies and Suffers
But count as expounders of rubbish that's old.

And cease upon that House to gaze.

Upon the London Albert Hall,

And gaze where Ireland’s glory lies.
Cath.——Oh, what a gang is out to block
The efforts of my stalwart stock.
Skeffy.—A proper gang to free the Gael
By hanging on to England’s tail.
On men, like me, your hopes abide,
So on my whiskers look with pride,
And think how changed might be your fate
Had Skeffy lived in 'ninety-eight.
The stalwart stock is at your back
Tn Hairy Fairy, crank and quack,
Whose hopes on RRedmond do not hang,

The first term of the Dublin College of Modern Trish,
20 Kildare Street, closed on Saturday last. The atten-
dance throughout the term was remarkably high, and
great progress has been made by the students in all the
classes. The elementary class has been put through
o course of instruction on the sounds of Irish, accom-
panied by drill on the individual sounds and combina-
tions of sounds, and through a course of direct method
and reading lessons. Though most of the students
did not know any Irish when they joined the class in
October, the whole class is now reading Asop’s Fables
as part of its regular work. The intermediate class
has done more advanced work of the same type. Very
advanced grammar, phonetics, ete., are done in Father
O’Nolan’s class, including a thorough analysis of the
language of Séadna. Condn Maol has dealt with three
centuries of Irish history in his courSe of lectures, and

~ Mr. O’Rahilly’s research class has read Seaghan Cla-

rach’s poems and prosc work, ‘“ An Giolla Deacair.
The second term will open on Monday, January 5th.

How do the Irish Colleges stand with regard to Trish
games? Of course we all know that St. Colman’s Col-
lege, Fermoy, is all right. How about the various
Seminarics in thecountry, and other Colleges? All
these Colleges, or nearly all of them, feed Maynooth
with students. In Maynooth there are, roughly, four
field games—Hurling, Gaelic Football, Soccer and
Rugby. Hurling, which came into Maynooth about
1900, the year the LEADER started, is immensely
popular, and has come to stay, and is at least three
times as well supported as any of the other games
mentioned. Gaelic football is fairly strong, at least as
strong, if not stronger, than Soccer or Rugby.
Soccer was at one time the game in Maynooth, but it

_ has been, and is, steadily on the wane, and its chief

supporters are Elphin, Derry, and Down and Connor
men, and the Northern dioceses generally, though to
a lesser extent. Rugby is played by fewest students,
but is, however, well organised, and those who play
it are very sticky on its behalf. After Baster
tennis is played- by nearly everyone in Maynooth,

and almost every diocese has a tennis court. The
reason, naturally, for the popularity of tennis after
Taster is the fine weather, and also the fact that the
students are studying very hard, and so all sorts of

Now, whatever games a student plays in his
Diocesan College he is likely to continue at when he
goos to Maynooth. Thus Armagh College men usually
‘play Soccer, while Meath men from St. Finian’s Col-
lege are generally Gaels. Kilmore is Gaelic, Raphoe
Soccer, and Dublin is everything; Ossory and Ferns
are Gaelic, and Kildare is, to a great extent, Rugby.
The Southern diocéses are largely Gaelic. Before the
opening of the new Seminary in Galway, Ballinasloe
College was the chief place from which Clonfert and
Galway students for Maynooth came from, and
Rugby was introduced into Ballinasloe some years
ago by a lay professor, and it is, we believe, largely in
consequence that Galway and Clonfert men are keen
on Rugby in Maynooth ; they are also keen on hurling.
Once you hear a student has been at Rockwell or
Blackrock you may be certain that he is a Rugbyite.

The great importance of Maynooth students playing
Irish games is that they are for the Irish mission, and
more especially because it is some of them who will
be the aathorities at the Diocesan Colleges, and the
Superiors of a College have great power in the matter
of games. Even though Fermoy is in a great hurling
county, we have no doubt that the position of hurling
in that famous Irish College owes a vast deal to the
enthusiasm of the President, Canon Barrett, no mean
hurler himself. If the Superiors of all these Diocesan
Colleges favoured Irish games, in due time such of
their students as went to Maynooth would play only
Irish games. Perhaps some of our readers interested
in Irish Colleges and games might-like to discuss this
matter a little further.

THI MIND.

But let us be fair to the philosopher of change. If
he treats omniscient intelligence with scant courtesy,
his object is not to exalt the human at the expense of
the divine. Intelligence has no part whate in his
system. He regards the intellect as & mere by-product
of evolution created to guide men in the practical
affairs of life, but hopelessly cut off by nature and
function from all relationship with things as they really
ure.

To know the real we must put intelligence aside and
fix our attention on the faculty which alone can
pace with the rapid movements of an ecver
world. This is intuition which M. Bergson dis
in one of those happy moments of exaltation when
vision is peculiarly clear. It is the complement of the
Kinetic view of the world that he defends; intuition in
the Bergsonian sense has nothing in common with the
operation of the mind by which it perceives the truth
of things without reasoning or anal This mental
function is confined within narrow. limits and extends
only to self-evident propositions about which there can
be no question or mauner of doubt. According to
Bergson it is neither mind nor intellect, noi t an act
of intelligence, but an organic sense; an instinct that
brings us into close touch with things, and penetrates
to the core of reality. It enables us to feel and sym-
pathise with nature, which is too swift and energetic
to be comprehended by the slow methods hitherto
observed by the intellectuals: . In no part of his philo-
sophy is M. Bergson more obscure than here, a fact
admitted by some of his most enthusiastic admirers.
This is strange in view of the importance he attaches
to intuition. Is the obscurity deliberate? ‘It is cer- -
tainly suspicious. .One would expect a singularly clear
exposition of the principle which is admittedly the
main factor in a system that pretends to be original.

From his own point of view he may be wise in hiding
from his friends the true .inwardness of his plan, but
he can hardly expect this reticence will satisfy others
who fail to appreciate his wisdom and see nothing in
his system except rhetoric and groundless assumptions.
If intuition, being in no .way connected with mind,
stands for anything it can only mean the consciousness
that necessarily accompanies. eyery form,of sensation;
in this acceptation of the term it is a.stern xeality on
which psychologists lay much, stress, though: for reasons
very different from those assigned by M. Bergson. . They

M. Bergson is free to adopt the words he considers

attain real knowledge we cry: Halt! the procedure
reminds us of the advice a professor of economics once
gave a farmer who consulted him as to the best way
of securing a constant supply of honey—the professor
replied—why, always keep a bee.

M. Bergson’s method is like the professor’s advice.
It is silly; but, unlike the shrewd farmer, who, no
doubt laughed in his sleeve at the professor, Bergson's
followers believe in him. He is listened to with rapt
attention by the groups of stud who flock to the
lecture-hall to hear him. He alv eaks in French,
and rumour has it that many of his hearers, who do not
understand the language, ave nevertheless convinced
of the truth of his teaching. Obviously they are not
carried away by the beauty and richness of his style.
Possibly it is owing to the keenness of their intuition,
or more probably to the personality of the lecturer;
a winning manner has irre: ble charms even among
the elite.

Those of us who have not had the privilege of hearing
him can only judge M. Bergson's system by reading
it in cold print. We are at a loss, for many of its
altractions must have evaporated in the press, while
everyone knows that a translation, however excellent,
often-misses the finer qualities of the original. How-
ever, making due allowance for the deteriorating pro-
cess through which it has passed, and assuming that
the kernel has reached us, we are tempted to ask why
M. Bergson has not devised some means of bringing
his philosophy within the compass of our brothers in
the animal kingdom. On his own showing, they
possess better developed instinets than man exhibits,
and besides, they are not hampered by intelligence
which rather tends to lead us astray.  Ants, in parti-
cular, would be successful disciples, while our next-
door neighbour—separated from us by a single link—
should rival Bergson himself, for he has all the perfec-
tions of inferior creatures and suffers none of the
inconveniences caused by the presence of mind. It is
really a pity M. Bergson overlooked the consequences
of his theory, especially those which tell against him:

Syndicalists, however; were on the alert, and were not
slow in drawing their'own conclusions from the philo-
sophy of change. It was the very thing they wanted,
and they hailed its author as the scientific champion
of their cause. -His principles justified their wildest
dreams of social reform. ~ For M. Bergson feeling is

- the norm of truth and morals, ethical considerations
' go by the board. These views fit in. nicely with the
* social programme. Society is rotten and requires a
complete overhauling.  Syndicalists feel that revolu-
tionary methods—strikes and sabotage—are the best
way of accomplishing:this end; and under sanction of
Bergson’s teaching proceed to put them into practice.
Sorel, a notorious Socialist, is disseminating Bergson's
~doctrine among the working classes in Irance, and
here in Dublin a similar propaganda is preached

I HAVE long been of the opinion that Ulster””

would be the betler of that projecied march to
Cork. The exercise would harden the marchers physi-
cally and would tend to soften them humanly—two
things they need. [ myself, though I am 'nob an
 Ulster ”” man, avail myself of every opportunity that
presents itself to get even as far southward as Dublin.
Tt does one good to go. Dublin and its people melt my
Trish blood in their sunny smiles and send it tingling
through my veins at such a rate that it doesn’t congeal
again till T have been fully a week back in Belfast. 1
was in Dublin for the Kerry-Wexford match, and that
is why T am writing this.

Now, I detest and loathe those ‘ Up Kerry,”  Up
Wexford” cries our Gaelic players have borrowed from
England, and so the reader will understand what good
humour I must have been in when I say that I bore
with equanimity the sight that met me in O’Connell
Street, of the manhood of Kerry and Wexford, wearing
in their hats horribly vulgar cards with ‘‘ Up Kerry "
and ‘“ Up Wexford " vulgarly printed on them. T bore
the sight with equanimity, I say, but as I
followed the procession of cards to Jones'’s
Road I Lkept wondering in my own mind
why on earth Dr. Hyde doesn’t quietly whisper
to his friends, the G.A.A. authorities, that they should
have Irish cries—'‘ Ciappaige Abu '’ or the like—on
banners or badges, and throw those dirty English cards
into the fire. A word from the genial Cpaotdin would,
1 am sure, be sufficient, and I for one—there are
thousands of others—wish he would speak the word.
It is a positive disgrace, to Kerry especially, to be Up-
ing at this time of the day.

I am nob going to describe the play,-nor even to com-
ment on it. What I want to comment on is the crowd
that witnessed the play. I wonder could you find in all
the world such another ocean of hearty humanity
swayed by such manful emotion and welling with such
healthy good humour as those big matches bring to-
gether in Dublin? I doubt if you could. You could
see oceans of humanity at football matches in England
and even in Belfast, but you would prefer, especially
if you had a lady in your company, to look out upon
those oceans from the shore and not mix with them.

THE PERSECUTION OF POLAND.
By R. J. Kenny, B.L.

AN Anglo-Polish 1:\dy., Miss Malecka, visited Dublin
last week and delivered an interesting lecture on
her treatment in a Russian prison. She is the daughter
of a distinguished medical doctor in London who was
himself a Polish patriot in the 'sixties and who fought
bravely and nobly for his country and suffered for his
patriotism. He is now a naturalised British subject,
and has been so for over thirty years. Hig gifted
daughter last year, keenly sympathising with her fel-
low-countrymen’s struggles and strivings, went to Cra-
cow and Warsaw, and to other cities of her divided
country. Poland, as it once existed, is now cut up
pet-ween Austria, Pri a and Russia, and that partition
is the blackest act of spoliation in the history of Bu-
rope. Miss Malecka was arrested and imprisoned and
sentenced to. four years’ detention in g fortress, but
fortunately liberated as she claimed the privileges of
British citizenship. The law, however, does not allow
a Russian citizen to denaturalise himself without the
consent of the Russian Government, and advantage
was t:tl»{en of that fact to detain her, because her father
was still a subject of Russia they contended, but the
Foreign Office prevailed, and Sir BEdward Grey got her
release.  What made her visit interesting to Irish-
men is the traditional sympathy of Ireland with Po-
land and of Poland with Ireland. These two countries
are so similar in many respects, their fortunes have
been so much alike, their sufferings and strivings alike,
that Poland’s subjection to alien domination makes the
story of her wrongs familiar to us as our own. We
always deeply sympathised with Poland and have ad-
mired the brave spirit with which she endured her
wrongs and bore up against the most cruel injustice
continuing to this very day in Russian and Prussian
Poland. But of one fact we are confident, and that
is that a people who gave and give such proofs of vigour
and vitality is one that cannot be crushed by oppression.
The Poles are increasing in numbers despite all this
oppression and also despite the inroads by emigration,
& serious and steady drain upon their population as
upon ours. They possess the qualities of a nation that
will sooner or later assert itself. The two greatest
empires of modern times, Germany and Russia, are
uneasy because of Polish discontent. Every device
that ingenuity could suggest is used by Prussia to re-
strict the growth of the Polish people, to prevent them
possessing land in their own country. TLaws have been
passed giving powers to the Government to buy up
compulsorily the estates held by Poles so that they may
be planted with Germans. Russia is equally ill at
ease because she withholds from the Poles the common
rights of citizenship.  Both countries proseribe the
Polish language, but in vain.
iss Malecka was imprisoned because she dared to
visit her father’s native land and happened without her
knowledge to be while there visited by a suspected
‘“ political °* or Socialist. Her trial was a travesty of
justice. She was accused by an informer of being pre-
sent at a Socialist meeting at a time when she proved
by the testimony of her employer, a German baron and
two other independent witnesses, she was actually en-
gaged in his household and living there as a governess.
But the informer was believed, as usual, and she was
gentenced to four years’ imprisonment, but liberated
only because she was a British citizen. In the prison
with her were geveral unfortunate persons who were
gome for five and more years awaiting their trial and

. who probably were innocent. This is a sample of

Russian law in these days, and it is little wonder there-
fore that a people so ill-treated should desire a thange.
The extent and intensity of the Polish question even in

“those empires of millions is seen by a comparison of
~ the population statistics. Tn 1910 there were 24,421,205
- Poles (all but 700,000 Catholics) thus distributed.

Over twenty-two millions in Furope are a consider-
able factor and one*that cannot be disregarded. If any
Buropean complications ensue in which Germany and
Russia find themselves seriously involved it is clear
that the rights of a down-trodden nationality such as
the Poles will have to be acknowledged and must as-
serb themsclves, but it is to be hoped in the interests
of common justice that Germany and Russia will not
wait for the coercion of such circumstances to do what
they should do in time—do justice to the Poles. It
cannot be their interest to keep in a state of discon-
tent and unrest a highly gifted and intellectual people
like the Poles, a people showing by their high morality
and their many excellent qualities that they well de-
serve to be given the highest form of political freedom,
and that they should not be kept in servitude and under
the lash of the tyranny now exercised over them with
such relentless cruelty. The tactics of terrorism should
be obsolete in these days of democratic freedom. There
is no justification for continuing in oppression a noble
race that have all the attributes of freedom and all the
qualifications of freemen. A nation that has given
Iurope its sweetest and most entrancing music, one
that has produced more musicians and finer and more
enduring music than relatively any other nation should
be treated as ordinary citizens in these countries which
by a series of cruel injustice got control over her des-
tinies.

Of the many black deeds on the page of European

history the blackest is undoubtedly the partition of
Poland in 1772, when three States, contrary to all prin-
ciples of international morality and common justice cut
up that fair land and divided the entire country between
themselves as so many highwaymen would distribute
their booty. This was the political crime which
a German, Professor Sybel, has characterised as the
greatest crime in history. The Treaty of Vienna
of 1815 secured the rights of the Poles, but
every right so secured was violated since and is vio-
ated to-day. Poland may be in a sense a negligible
actor because of its division, but the Poles, because of
their solidarity and strength are not a negligible quan-
tity in Buropean problems, and sooner or later they
will assert themselves and make themselves felt as
they have certainly succeeded in making themselves
feared. Coercion is always the result and outcome of
ear, and when Russia and Prussia to-day coerce Po-
land and refuse to let Poles possess land even in their
own counfry but strive to dispossess them as Prussia
does so discreditably in the province of Posen, it shows
clearly that the Poles are feared and not despised.
In Russia, and especially in Russian Poland, no citi-
zen, from the meanest to the greatest, is safe from
molestation and robbery at the hands of bandits who
swarm these countries and revive there the worst tra-
ditions of robbery and violence on the highways and
even in the streets of the towns. Trom official statis-
tics it appears that between 1907 and 1913 there were
no less than 88,389 cases of bandittism in the whole
empire, in which 8,804 persons were killed by bandits
and 9,256 injured. Of these 13,891 were private citi-
zens and the rest public officials and functionaries. In
the majority of cases ab least a considerable proportion
oceurred in Russian Poland, where the safety of the
citizen is in still greater danger. By a strange irony
u number of the police are said to be in connivance with.
these illegal proceedings. One would imagine that the
Russian Government would be better occupied pubting
down bandits than vainly trying 'to suppress Polish
patriotism; but they seem to direct all’their energie
to the one and neglect the elementary duties of a S
towards its subiects.

In Germany so far is freedom of speec
that the explorer Amundsen was prevent

by law of using their own language, and they are forced
